truthAudit.Summary: Audit of Run 2 findings against the 20 true vulnerabilities reveals that only 3 of the 20 vulnerabilities were correctly identified in the audited answer. The previous answer found Format-String Injection (SC-V3-001), Command Injection via Config Validation (SC-V3-004), and Stack Buffer Overflow (SC-V3-005) as full matches. One additional finding was reported under a different classification (SC-V3-013). The remaining 16 vulnerabilities were missed entirely. No false positives were identified in the previous answer; all reported findings had valid code evidence.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Format String Vulnerability in Log Message"\n"evidence": "std::string active_format = runtime_log_format();\\nprintf(active_format.c_str(), user_input);"
Items.Notes: The previous answer correctly identified the format string vulnerability at lines 237-239 where printf uses a user-controlled format string from runtime_log_format() directly.
The previous answer did not mention the integer overflow in factorial computation (lines 316-318) where result *= i uses a long long that can overflow for large inputs.

The previous answer did not identify the use-after-free in Memory-Pool cleanup (lines 817-826) where scratch buffers allocated from g_memory_pool are used in async tasks after cleanup may have freed them.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Command Injection via Config Validation"\n"evidence": "std::string validator_cmd = get_setting(\"validator_path\", \"/usr/bin/validator\");\\nstd::string config_file = get_setting(\"config_file\", \"default.conf\");\\nstd::string command = validator_cmd + \" \" + config_file;\\nint result = system(command.c_str());"
Items.Notes: The previous answer correctly identified command injection via config validation at lines 564-568 where user-controlled config values are concatenated and passed to system().

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Buffer Overflow via Unsafe String Copy"\n"evidence": "if (expression.length() >= sizeof(input_buffer_)) {\\n    strcpy(input_buffer_, expression.c_str());"
Items.Notes: The previous answer correctly identified the stack buffer overflow at lines 831-833 where strcpy is used without bounds checking when expression length exceeds input_buffer_ size.
The previous answer did not identify the race condition in ThreadSafeCounter (lines 482-487) where volatile long long is read without lock before incrementing.
The previous answer did not identify the logic bomb in authentication (lines 592-598) where EMERGENCY_OVERRIDE bypasses auth after 5 failed attempts.
The previous answer did not identify path traversal in configuration loading (lines 542-543) where config_dir_ + filename is concatenated without sanitization.
The previous answer did not identify heap overflow in Expression Parser (lines 368-377) where strcpy is used with a 256-byte buffer without bounds checking.
The previous answer did not identify hardcoded administrator credentials (line 73) where ADMIN_SECRET is defined as SC_ENT_2025_AUTH.
The previous answer did not identify weak PRNG for session tokens (lines 636-640) where std::mt19937 with uniform_int_distribution(1000, 9999) generates only 4-digit tokens.
The previous answer did not identify thread-unsafe timestamp cache in logging (lines 230-232) where static std::tm cached_time is updated without synchronization.

Items.PreviousOutputQuote: "Command Injection in Emergency Override Audit Logging"\n"evidence": "#ifdef _WIN32\\n    const char* temp_dir = std::getenv(\"TEMP\");\\n    std::string audit_path = std::string(temp_dir ? temp_dir : \".\") + \"\\\\supercalc_emergency.log\";\\n    std::string command = \"cmd /C echo Admin override logged >> \" + audit_path;"
Items.Notes: The previous answer found this vulnerability but classified it as command injection rather than insecure temporary file handling. The evidence matches lines 624-631 which is the same code, so the finding was valid but misclassified.
The previous answer did not identify persistent authentication state (lines 579-582, 635-640) where authenticated_ and session_token_ are never cleared on logout.
The previous answer did not identify unhandled exception in variable parsing (lines 728-730) where std::stod(tokens[3]) can throw without proper error handling.
The previous answer did not identify integer underflow in Memory-Pool block split (lines 129-134) where previous_size - size - SPLIT_METADATA_BYTES can underflow if remaining <= 32.

The previous answer did not identify unbounded recursion in parser (lines 381-433) where parse_expression calls parse_term which calls parse_factor which can call parse_expression again without depth limits.
The previous answer did not identify data race in result cache (lines 446-452) where result_cache_ is accessed without proper synchronization when thread_pool_ enqueues evaluation tasks.
The previous answer did not identify TOCTOU in config loading (lines 543-547) where std::filesystem::exists is checked then sleep_for is called before opening the file.
The previous answer did not identify integer division by zero in command routing (lines 751-753) where 100 / static_cast<int>(token_length) can divide by zero if token_length is 0.
